title: Divergence 4: brainstorm → implementation auto-transition (hand off to run-queue by default)
description:
# Context

Observed in the current lu3 session (and foreshadowed by lu3.1 / lu3.2): after brainstorming completes and the bead is marked `implementation-ready`, the natural flow of the agent — combined with superpowers momentum (TDD, writing-plans, subagent-driven-development) — pulls the user straight into implementation in the **same session**.

The `implement-feature` and `fix-bug` formulas are explicitly intended to be processed by the `run-queue` skill in a **dedicated session** (see beads.md Session Separation rule). Continuing in-session defeats that separation and makes run-queue redundant.

# Root cause (hypothesis)

1. `brainstorm-bead.formula.toml` finalize step says "It will be picked up by the next run-queue cycle, **or** you can start it manually with the implement-bead skill" — the word "or" reads as a choice rather than a hand-off signal.
2. `start-bead` Route C has no post-brainstorm pause: after the brainstorm formula completes, the agent is still "on" the bead with no explicit boundary telling it to stop.
3. `start-bead` Route A fires unconditionally when `implementation-ready` is present. This is the actual mechanism by which in-session continuation happens — the agent re-enters start-bead and hits A silently, bypassing the finalize-step message. (Surfaced by adversarial review of this bead's own spec.)
4. `implement-bead`'s "When to Use" lists "User explicitly says 'implement bead xyz' after brainstorming is complete" — this is too permissive; completing brainstorming in the same session is easily misread as implicit authorization.
5. Superpowers skills (writing-plans → subagent-driven-development etc.) create downstream pull even after lu3.2's partnership fences — they flow from brainstorming unless explicitly stopped at the bead boundary.
6. `run-queue` has no mirror guard against the opposite drift (a run-queue session brainstorming a spec gap inline) beyond existing Red Flags.

# Edits required

## 1. `src/plugins/beads/.beads/formulas/brainstorm-bead.formula.toml`

In the finalize step's Report-to-the-user block (currently lines 186–194), replace the existing report text with a hand-off-by-default message while PRESERVING the `Acceptance criteria: [list]` echo and the subsequent `bd mol burn` step (step 4). The new message:

    Report to the user:
      "Bead {{bead-id}} is now implementation-ready.
       Acceptance criteria: [list]

       This session stops here. Do NOT continue to implementation in this
       session unless you explicitly authorize it. The default path is:
       another agent (run-queue, in a dedicated session) picks up the
       implementation.

       If you want to implement now in the current session instead, say so
       explicitly ("implement it now", "do lu3.N next", etc.) and I will
       invoke `implement-bead`."

    Then STOP. Do not invoke implement-bead unless the user replies with
    explicit authorization within this session.

## 2. `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/start-bead/SKILL.md` — Route A

Add an authorization gate to Route A. Replace "Action: invoke the `implement-bead` skill directly." with:

    Action: check session context. If the `implementation-ready` label was
    applied in THIS session (e.g. the brainstorm formula just finalized this
    bead), STOP and require explicit user authorization before invoking
    `implement-bead`. Otherwise invoke `implement-bead` directly.

    The default in a just-brainstormed scenario is hand-off to run-queue.
    Ask the user only when intent is unclear; silent continuation is not
    permitted.

## 3. `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/start-bead/SKILL.md` — Route C

Append a Hand-off block at the bottom of Route C (after the execution loop and failure-mode section):

    ### Hand-off: stop at implementation-ready

    When the brainstorm formula completes and the bead has `implementation-ready`
    and `brainstormed` labels, STOP. The default path is: another agent
    (run-queue, in a dedicated session) picks up the implementation.

    Do NOT invoke `implement-bead` in this session unless the user:
    - Explicitly directs you to ("implement it now", "do X next"), OR
    - Explicitly agrees when you ask.

    Do NOT re-invoke `start-bead` on this bead in this session — that would
    route through Route A and bypass this hand-off.

    Asking is acceptable when the user's intent is unclear. Silent assumption
    that brainstorming means "also implement" is not.

Append a Red Flag row to the existing Red Flags table:

    | "Brainstorming is done, I'll implement next as a natural continuation" | No. Default is hand-off to run-queue. Stop unless explicitly authorized. |

## 4. `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/implement-bead/SKILL.md`

Tighten the "When to Use" list. Replace:

    - User explicitly says "implement bead xyz" after brainstorming is complete

with:

    - User explicitly and affirmatively authorizes implementation in this
      session (e.g. "implement it now", "yes, go"). Completing brainstorming
      in the same session is NOT implicit authorization.

Append a Red Flag row to the existing Red Flags table:

    | "Brainstorming finished cleanly, so the user must want implementation" | No. Hand off to run-queue by default. Ask or wait for explicit authorization. |

## 5. `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/run-queue/SKILL.md`

Append a mirror Red Flag row to the existing Red Flags table, guarding the opposite-direction drift (a run-queue session brainstorming inline):

    | "The bead isn't quite implementation-ready, I'll brainstorm the gap inline" | No. run-queue processes implementation-ready beads only. If the spec has gaps, flag the bead for re-brainstorming and move on. |

Match the wording/tone to the file's existing Red Flag phrasing.

## 6. `src/plugins/beads/.claude/rules/beads.md`

In the Session Separation section (currently lines 91–98), add a new bullet:

    **Post-brainstorm hand-off**: Any bead newly labeled `implementation-ready`
    in the current session is a hand-off candidate by default — regardless of
    how the label was set (brainstorm formula, manual `bd label add`, or
    imported). Implementation runs in a separate run-queue session.
    Continuing to implementation in the current session requires explicit
    user authorization per session.

# Design decisions

- **Default is hand-off, not implementation.** Aligns with the Session Separation rule already in beads.md: brainstorming session ≠ implementation session. Continuing in-session is an authorized exception, not the path of least resistance.
- **Ask-or-wait, not always-wait.** Asking the user at the boundary is fine ("Want me to implement now, or hand off to run-queue?"). What's not fine is silent continuation.
- **Reinforce at SIX layers** (formula finalize step, start-bead Route A gate, start-bead Route C hand-off block, implement-bead When-to-Use, run-queue mirror Red Flag, beads.md Session Separation). Defense-in-depth because the momentum toward implementation is strong.
- **Rule applies to the label, not the producer.** The beads.md bullet is phrased around "newly labeled `implementation-ready` in this session" so it catches hand-labeled and imported beads, not just brainstorm-bead output.
- **Route A is the primary breach site.** The finalize-step message alone is insufficient — once the label is applied, Route A silently re-routes any subsequent start-bead invocation straight into implement-bead. The Route A gate closes that path.

# Acceptance criteria

- `brainstorm-bead.formula.toml` finalize step's user-facing report explicitly says the session stops here and run-queue picks up by default; preserves the `Acceptance criteria: [list]` echo; preserves the `bd mol burn` step (step 4); and closes with an imperative "Then STOP" instruction to the agent.
- `start-bead` Route A has an authorization gate that blocks silent invocation of `implement-bead` when the `implementation-ready` label was applied in the current session.
- `start-bead` Route C has a Hand-off block at the end with the "default is STOP" rule, explicitly prohibits same-session re-entry through `start-bead`, and has a Red Flag row against natural-continuation bias.
- `implement-bead` When-to-Use tightens the brainstorming-continuation path and adds a matching Red Flag row.
- `run-queue` SKILL.md has a mirror Red Flag row guarding against brainstorming inline in a run-queue session.
- `beads.md` Session Separation has a new Post-brainstorm hand-off bullet phrased around the label (not the producer), so it catches manual and imported cases.
- No regressions to existing formula/skill/rule content; all edits are additive or replace exact text as spec'd.
- Build / typecheck / tests pass (vacuous for this docs-only repo — manual review of the six files is the real bar).

# Notes

- Discovered during session `eed3f46f` (the lu3 work itself) — the session went brainstorm → implementation immediately because no boundary existed. Exactly the pattern this bead fixes.
- Related to lu3.1 (materialize formula steps), lu3.2 (partnership contract fences methodology skills), and lu3.3 (delivery action categories). This one specifically targets the brainstorm→implement seam inside the bead lifecycle.
- Root-level template files (`src/user/.claude/rules/delegation.md`, root CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md templates) are intentionally out of scope. This is a plugin-scoped fix.
- Implementer note: any `\"` sequences in this description are markdown-storage artifacts. Emit plain double-quotes (`"`) in the actual file edits.
- Red Flag rows are appended to their existing tables; GFM does not require column alignment, so visual alignment is not a criterion.
- Dogfooding: this bead itself is subject to the rule it defines — when brainstorming of this bead completes, hand off to run-queue in a dedicated session; do not continue in-session without explicit authorization.

title: Split ralf-it into ralf-review + ralf-implement; push outer-workflow out of both
description:
# Context

`ralf-it` is currently a top-level skill in `src/user/.agents/skills/ralf-it/`. Session audit `c06c7b55` (Divergence 3 analysis) identified two problems with this form:

1. **Auto-invocation magnetism**: as a skill, `ralf-it` competes with bead-driven workflows the same way `writing-plans`, `executing-plans`, and `subagent-driven-development` do. It gets matched by the agent's "what skill fits this task?" scanner and pulled in even when a bead formula should own the flow.

2. **Over-specifies outer-workflow concerns**: its SKILL.md declares `using-git-worktrees` and `finishing-a-development-branch` as "REQUIRED SUB-SKILLS" — but those are outer-workflow concerns that conflict with bead formulas when `ralf-it` is invoked from inside a formula step. The formula already owns worktree isolation (via `implement-bead` setup) and delivery (via a later formula step).

The underlying RALF methodology — red-green-refactor iteration, fresh-eyes subagent review cycles, quality-gate passes — is valuable. The question is: **what FORM should it take to eliminate the friction without losing the methodology?**

# Open design questions

## Q1: What form should ralf-it take?

- **Skill (current)**: auto-invokable, magnetic, competes with formulas. Problematic.
- **Command (`/ralf-it`)**: user-invoked only, no auto-match. Eliminates magnetism. Still usable standalone.
- **Agent (or set of agents)**: dispatched by orchestrator. Slots cleanly inside formula steps as "dispatch the ralf-it agent."
- **Hybrid**: keep the methodology as a reusable reference (doc or molecule/formula), invoke via different entry points for different contexts.

## Q2: How do bead formulas embed the RALF methodology?

If we restructure ralf-it out of skill form, bead formulas must explicitly embed the RALF pattern. Places this matters:

- **`brainstorm-bead.formula.toml`** — already has a `ralf-spec-review` step (fresh-eyes review of written spec). Demonstrates the inner-only pattern. ✓
- **`implement-feature.formula.toml`** — needs a RALF loop step for the implementation (red-green-refactor + fresh-eyes refinement cycles + quality gate)
- **`fix-bug.formula.toml`** — same pattern as implement-feature, plus systematic-debugging up front

Two sub-options:
- **Inline the RALF pattern as multiple formula steps** (decompose it)
- **Create a RALF sub-formula/molecule** that other formulas reference (composable)

## Q3: What about non-bead, standalone use?

A user running `/ralf-it` on a quick bug fix without creating a bead still needs the full methodology. Whatever form we pick must support standalone use without forcing a bead workflow.

## Q4: Outer-workflow concerns

Regardless of form, ralf-it should be **inner-methodology only** — it iterates on CODE, not on worktrees or delivery. Who owns the outer loop?

- If called from a formula: the formula
- If called standalone: the user (or the command's documentation tells them what to set up first)

# Constraints

- Must not break standalone use (power users running `/ralf-it this bug` without a bead should still get the methodology)
- Must eliminate auto-match magnetism so it stops competing with bead formulas
- Must not require agents to remember "don't use ralf-it when on a bead" (Divergence 2 proved that rule-only guardrails leak)
- Existing `brainstorm-bead`'s `ralf-spec-review` step is the reference implementation of the inner-only pattern

# Trigger

Not implementation-ready. Requires brainstorming to decide form. Start after:
- `agents-config-lu3.1` is merged AND
- `agents-config-lu3.2` is merged AND
- Observation period long enough to see whether ralf-it friction persists in practice

# Brainstorm inputs (for future session)

- Current SKILL.md structure: 9-step workflow including worktree setup (Step 3) and delivery handoff (Step 9)
- Other prompts in the dir: `foreign-agent-prompt.md`, `foreign-eyes-prompt.md`, `fresh-eyes-prompt.md`, `implementer-prompt.md` — these are the subagent prompts ralf uses
- Partnership contract precedent: `writing-plans`/`executing-plans`/`subagent-driven-development` chosen as "off-limits" — ralf-it currently evades that list because we didn't think to add it
- Related: agents-config-717 (risk-closer evaluation) — ralf-it reform could be one of the risk-closers

# Acceptance criteria (TBD during brainstorm)

- `bd decision` recorded on ralf-it's new form (skill / command / agent / hybrid) with rationale
- If restructuring: formulas updated to embed RALF methodology inline, or a reusable RALF molecule/formula created
- If keeping as skill: skill is cleaned to be workflow-agnostic (no REQUIRED outer-workflow skills), red flag added to prevent peer invocation on beads
- Standalone use path documented and preserved
- Build passes. Typecheck passes. Tests pass.

# Notes

- NOT blocked by lu3.1/lu3.2 (can start independently), but best run after those land so we can observe ralf-it behavior in the fixed pipeline
- This is a DESIGN question — route through `start-bead` → `brainstorm-bead` formula when ready
- Session `c06c7b55` flagged this as a cleaner fix than patching ralf-it's workflow declarations in place

notes:
# Spec: Re-evaluate `ralf-it` form — split into `ralf-review` + `ralf-implement` (agents-config-lu3.4)

## Summary

Split the current `ralf-it` skill into two explicit-invocation skills —
**`ralf-review`** (adversarial fresh-eyes passes over a target; read-only w.r.t. the
codebase) and **`ralf-implement`** (iterative same-working-copy implementation with
fresh-eyes cycles between passes). Push worktree and delivery responsibility
OUTSIDE both skills — they are inner methodology only. Teach
`implement-feature` / `fix-bug` formulas to dispatch `ralf-implement` selectively
based on a single `ralf:required` bead label set during brainstorming, with
`brainstorm-bead`'s existing `ralf-spec-review` step rewritten to dispatch
`ralf-review` against the spec document.

## Background

The current `ralf-it` skill:

- **Auto-matches aggressively** via its skill description, competing with bead-formula
  workflow.
- **Over-specifies outer workflow**: declares `superpowers:using-git-worktrees` and
  `superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch` as REQUIRED sub-skills.
- **Conflates two distinct behaviors** — iterative implementation AND adversarial
  review — into a single skill where the only separation is iteration number.
- **Docs/reality mismatch**: `src/plugins/beads/.claude/rules/beads.md` calls
  `implement-feature` / `fix-bug` "RALF-IT" — but their TOML is a linear completion
  gate with NO iteration, NO fresh-eyes subagent dispatch, NO convergence logic.
- **Peer-ban contradiction**: `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/implement-bead/SKILL.md`
  lists `ralf-it` alongside `executing-plans` / `subagent-driven-development` as a
  peer-banned skill. After this spec, RALF is dispatched BY formula steps — the
  ban must be reframed.

Beads domain expert counsel established that embedding RALF iteration as formula
primitives (conditional steps, loop-until, loop-range) fights the tool. RALF is
subagent-dispatch behavior — a quality behavior attached to specific formula
steps, not a workflow phase.

The two behaviors RALF blends naturally split along concern lines:

- **Review** — adversarial multi-pass fresh-eyes evaluation of a target
  (spec doc, design doc, code). Read-only w.r.t. the codebase. May write feedback
  to a doc. Applicable to specs, designs, or code.
- **Implement** — iterative refinement of code in the working copy. Writes code.
  Each cycle: implement → fresh-eyes review → evaluate → loop or converge.

Splitting yields cleaner consumers: `brainstorm-bead.ralf-spec-review` (already
exists) becomes a clean dispatch of `ralf-review`. `implement-feature.implement`
dispatches `ralf-implement` when warranted. Neither skill touches worktrees or PRs.

## Decision

**Two skills, both inner-methodology only**:

- **`ralf-review`**: dispatches one or more adversarial fresh-eyes subagents
  against a target. Read-only w.r.t. the codebase (may write feedback doc).
  Iterates up to a bounded cycle count; converges when findings are non-significant.
- **`ralf-implement`**: iterative same-working-copy implementation with
  fresh-eyes review passes between cycles. Writes code. Worktree is NOT its
  concern — caller arranges isolation if needed.

**Caller owns outer workflow**: worktree setup, PR creation, delivery. Whether
the caller is a bead formula step (formula owns the outer workflow) or a user
running `/ralf-implement …` (user arranges their own isolation, OR runs the
skill against their current working copy if that's what they want).

**Separation of concerns:**

- Beads track WHAT (unit of work, AC, state)
- Formulas encode workflow phases (intent → isolation → implement → gate → deliver → housekeep)
- Skills encode HOW quality behaviors work (`ralf-review` = adversarial multi-pass
  review; `ralf-implement` = iterative refinement loop)

**Signal design:**

- `ralf:required` label on a bead — canonical signal that formula steps should
  dispatch their corresponding RALF variant where applicable. Step-type routes:
  an `implement`-type step dispatches `ralf-implement`; a `review`-type step
  dispatches `ralf-review`. No separate labels per variant — the step is the
  disambiguator.
- `ralf:cycles=N` label — optional override for the invoked skill's
  `MAX_ITERATIONS`. Applies to whichever variant runs at the step.
- Labels are read at the step's dispatch boundary; changing labels
  mid-implementation has NO effect on the current run.
- **Setter contract (R15)**: any actor setting `ralf:cycles=N` MUST first
  `bd label remove` any existing `ralf:cycles=*` to prevent duplicates.
- Default values (`MAX_ITERATIONS`) live in each skill, not in formulas.

## Requirements

### R1. Split `ralf-it` into two skills; push outer-workflow OUT of both

- Create `src/user/.agents/skills/ralf-review/SKILL.md` — adversarial multi-pass
  review skill; read-only w.r.t. codebase; takes a target (spec/design/code)
  as argument
- Create `src/user/.agents/skills/ralf-implement/SKILL.md` — iterative
  same-working-copy implementation skill with fresh-eyes review cycles
- Retire `src/user/.agents/skills/ralf-it/` (delete directory OR leave only a
  short deprecation stub pointing at the successor skills — pick during rewrite)
- Neither skill requires `superpowers:using-git-worktrees` or
  `superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch` as sub-skills — worktree and
  delivery are the caller's concern
- Keep foreign-agent setup (`.ralf/` directory + Codex/Gemini dispatch) —
  inner tooling; distribute between the two skills as each needs it

### R2. Remove magnetism from both new skills

- Each skill's description begins with an explicit-invocation marker
  (e.g., "Explicit invocation only — …"); contains no auto-match trigger
  phrases ("use when executing", "use when implementing", "use when reviewing", etc.)
- Skill bodies include guard language: invoke only from explicit request,
  or from a formula step's dispatch contract — not as a peer of a bead workflow

### R3. Preserve RALF invariants in both skills

Four invariants apply to both skills, documented explicitly at the top of each:

- **Iteration** — multi-pass, bounded by `MAX_ITERATIONS`; not one-shot
- **Independence** — each cycle's subagent is brand-new (no prior cycle context);
  given the original target, not the previous cycle's summary
- **Adversarial posture** — seek gaps; never rubber-stamp
- **Convergence** — explicit termination when findings are non-significant OR
  budget exhausted

Additional per-skill preservation:

- **`ralf-review`**: the iteration pattern currently embedded in
  `brainstorm-bead.ralf-spec-review` (up to 2 cycles, early-exit on clean) is
  the reference
- **`ralf-implement`**: iteration-routing preserved (Codex iter 1, Gemini iter 2,
  pure Claude iter 3+); subagent-dispatch contract preserved (iteration count
  NOT disclosed; "may be incomplete" posture); foreign-agent failure degrades
  cleanly to pure fresh-eyes
- **Convergence escalation**:
  - **Standalone invocation**: each skill asks the user to continue if `MAX_ITERATIONS`
    is reached and last pass still found significant work
  - **Bead-driven invocation** (argument is a valid bead ID): skill calls
    `bd human <bead-id>` with a summary of remaining concerns; agent does NOT
    unilaterally extend

### R4. Argument/context handling — no worktree detection

Each skill accepts a target argument. Neither skill detects or creates worktrees:

- **`ralf-review`** — target can be: bead ID (reviews the bead's spec/description),
  file path (reviews the doc), or free text (reviews the described artifact).
  Treated as bead-driven if the argument is a valid bead ID AND `bd show <id>` succeeds.
- **`ralf-implement`** — target can be: bead ID (works against the bead's AC) or
  free text (works against a user-described goal). Same bead-driven detection.
- **No worktree precondition check**. If the caller wants isolation, the caller
  arranges it first. This preserves current standalone UX (the user's working
  copy is the working copy — no abort, no magic worktree creation).
- **Bead-driven mode** reads labels via `bd label list <id> --json`:
  - Presence check: `ralf:required` (gating happens at the formula step, not
    inside the skill — the formula decides to invoke)
  - `ralf:cycles=N`: positive integer, range 1..20
  - On multiple `ralf:cycles=N` labels (setter violated R15 or user manually
    added duplicates): warn, use skill default, recommend the user clean up
  - On malformed/out-of-range `ralf:cycles=N`: warn, use skill default

### R5. Single source of truth for defaults

- `MAX_ITERATIONS` default lives in each skill's SKILL.md; formulas never
  duplicate it. Each skill may pick its own default — `ralf-review` and
  `ralf-implement` need not share the number.
- Formulas read `ralf:cycles=N` label and pass to the invoked skill; skill
  falls back to its internal default when the label is absent
- Specific default values are skill-rewrite decisions, NOT spec decisions

### R6. `implement-feature.formula.toml` — `implement` step

Replace the current flat "Skills: superpowers:test-driven-development, then domain
skill" line with a label-driven skill-selection contract:

```
Check the bead's RALF signal BEFORE dispatching:

  bd label list {{bead-id}} --json

- If label `ralf:required` present:
    → invoke ralf-implement (pass {{bead-id}} as argument)
    ralf-implement owns the INNER quality loop only: iteration, independence,
    adversarial posture, convergence. It does NOT own worktree or PR creation
    — those belong to this formula. Optional cycle override via label
    `ralf:cycles=N`; skill default applies otherwise.

- Otherwise:
    → invoke superpowers:test-driven-development, then appropriate domain skill
      (typescript-developer, backend-developer, etc.)

Record the dispatch choice for audit:

  bd comments add {{bead-id}} "implement: dispatched <skill-name>"

Rules (apply to whichever skill is dispatched):
- Minimum viable implementation — write only what tests require
- No speculative features, no over-engineering
- No abstractions for hypothetical future requirements
```

**Mandated**: audit breadcrumb uses `bd comments add` (verified supported).
Do NOT use `bd update --notes` — replaces the spec.

### R7. `fix-bug.formula.toml` — `implement-fix` step

Identical label-driven skill-selection contract as R6. Consistency required.

### R8. `brainstorm-bead.formula.toml` — two changes

**R8a. Rewrite `ralf-spec-review` step to dispatch `ralf-review`:**

```
Dispatch ralf-review against this bead's spec document.

Skill: ralf-review
Target: {{bead-id}}

ralf-review runs bounded adversarial fresh-eyes cycles against the spec
(completeness, ambiguity, feasibility, consistency). It converges when
findings are non-significant or the cycle budget is exhausted. Review
the findings it reports; revise the spec if warranted (return to write-spec).
Run at most 2 spec review cycles before proceeding with remaining concerns
documented as open questions.
```

The step's behavior is preserved; what changes is that the inline review
logic is now encapsulated in the `ralf-review` skill, so the step body
shrinks to a dispatch.

**R8b. Add RALF triage to `finalize` step (before the existing label stamping):**

```
RALF triage — assess whether implementation warrants iterative refinement:

- If the spec touches any of: security-sensitive code, authentication/authorization,
  payment processing, data migration, architectural shift, multi-file coordinated
  change, or carries explicit user directive for RALF:
    → bd label add {{bead-id}} ralf:required
    → if a specific cycle count is warranted (differs from skill default):
        → bd label remove {{bead-id}} ralf:cycles=*   # R15: prevent duplicates
        → bd label add {{bead-id}} ralf:cycles=N

- Otherwise: proceed without RALF labels. Default implementation path is
  TDD + domain skill.
```

### R9. `resolve-pr-comments` boundary fix

- Remove all `ralf-it` invocation. Use `grep -n 'ralf-it'
  src/user/.agents/skills/resolve-pr-comments/SKILL.md` as authoritative
  locator (currently at lines 146, 281).
- Skill does NOT dispatch any RALF variant (neither `ralf-review` nor
  `ralf-implement`)
- Remove any implied or prescribed downstream steps (delivery, merge, cleanup)
- Scope the skill to pure PR comment resolution — nothing more
- If significant work on a comment is warranted, it is the caller's job to
  arrange that — the skill punts

### R10. `src/user/.claude/rules/delegation.md` rewrite

Current text: "Implementation → `ralf-it` skill (preferred for non-trivial work)"

Rewritten delegation rule reflects the two-skill split:

- Default implementation path: `superpowers:test-driven-development` +
  appropriate domain skill
- **`ralf-implement`** is opt-in via bead label `ralf:required` (bead-driven)
  OR explicit `/ralf-implement` invocation (standalone)
- **`ralf-review`** is used by formula review-type steps (e.g., `brainstorm-bead`'s
  `ralf-spec-review`) or invoked explicitly (`/ralf-review <target>`)
- Labels are read at the step's dispatch boundary; changing labels
  mid-implementation has NO effect on the current run
- Non-trivial is the NORM, not a trigger for RALF — most non-trivial work
  does not warrant `ralf-implement`

### R11. `src/plugins/beads/.claude/rules/beads.md` corrections

- Stop calling formulas "RALF-IT" (current text:
  `implement-feature — RALF-IT feature implementation`,
  `fix-bug — root-cause diagnosis + RALF-IT fix`). Corrected descriptions
  reflect the label-driven `ralf-implement` dispatch in the implement step;
  otherwise formulas are TDD + completion gate.
- ADD rows to the existing Bead Lifecycle and Labels table (do not replace
  the table — existing rows including `implementation-readied-session-<sid>`
  remain). Final expected table (6 rows):

  | Label | Set by | Meaning |
  |-------|--------|---------|
  | `brainstormed` | `brainstorm-bead.finalize` | Spec written and reviewed |
  | `implementation-ready` | `brainstorm-bead.finalize` | Ready for implement-bead / run-queue |
  | `implementation-readied-session-<sid>` | `brainstorm-bead.finalize` | Session marker for Route A gating |
  | `human` | Any agent via `bd human <id>` | Needs human attention |
  | `ralf:required` | `brainstorm-bead.finalize` (or manual) | Formula step dispatches `ralf-implement` (implement step) or `ralf-review` (review step) |
  | `ralf:cycles=N` | `brainstorm-bead.finalize` (or manual) | Override `MAX_ITERATIONS` (optional; skill default otherwise). Setters MUST remove any existing `ralf:cycles=*` first. |

### R12. `bd decision` record

- Title: "RALF split into ralf-review + ralf-implement; both inner-methodology only"
- Notes: Two-worlds collapsed to one family of two skills. `ralf-review`
  (read-only adversarial multi-pass) and `ralf-implement` (iterative
  same-working-copy implementation). Worktree / delivery are caller concerns,
  not skill concerns. Label-driven dispatch at formula steps via single
  `ralf:required` label; step type routes to the correct variant.
  `MAX_ITERATIONS` defaults owned by each skill. `ralf:cycles=N` duplicates
  prevented by setter-enforced cleanup (R15). Rationale: expert counsel
  established in-formula iteration primitives fight the tool; RALF is
  subagent-dispatch behavior; splitting mirrors the natural concern boundary;
  one skill family prevents drift; labels give queryable, compaction-surviving
  signal.

### R13. Subagent prompt template distribution

Current templates under `src/user/.agents/skills/ralf-it/`:

- `implementer-prompt.md` → move to `ralf-implement/`; strip the outer-workflow
  directive at line 7 (`isolation: "worktree"`)
- `fresh-eyes-prompt.md` → duplicate into both `ralf-implement/` and
  `ralf-review/` with appropriate scope (review-focus vs implement-cycle);
  strip `Work from: [worktree directory]` directives (currently at line 72 in
  the source)
- `foreign-eyes-prompt.md` → primarily for `ralf-implement/`; may be reused
  by `ralf-review/` if foreign-agent review of spec/design docs is desired
  (decide during rewrite)
- `foreign-agent-prompt.md` → move alongside foreign-eyes wherever it ends up

### R14. `implement-bead` skill peer-ban reconciliation

`src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/implement-bead/SKILL.md:169` currently lists
`ralf-it` alongside `executing-plans` / `subagent-driven-development` as a
peer-banned skill. After this spec:

- **Remove** `ralf-it` from that peer-ban row entirely (leaving
  `executing-plans` and `subagent-driven-development`)
- **Add a NEW row** clarifying: `ralf-review` and `ralf-implement` are
  dispatched BY formula steps (not invoked as peers). Invocation as a peer
  of the bead workflow is forbidden; invocation from within a formula step's
  dispatch contract is REQUIRED when `ralf:required` label is set.
- Verify no other text in `implement-bead/SKILL.md` contradicts this

### R15. Setter-enforced `ralf:cycles=*` deduplication

Any actor (formula step, skill, user) setting `ralf:cycles=N` MUST first
remove any existing `ralf:cycles=*` label:

```
bd label remove <bead-id> ralf:cycles=*    # remove any existing
bd label add    <bead-id> ralf:cycles=N    # add the new one
```

This is the contract; consumers treat multiple coexisting `ralf:cycles=*`
labels as user error (warn + skill default, per R4). Beads labels are pure
strings — the system does NOT overwrite on re-add (verified empirically:
`bd label add bead test:foo=1` then `bd label add bead test:foo=2` leaves
both labels on the bead). Setter discipline is how we enforce "single effective
value."

## Out of scope

- Native beads formula iteration primitives (sub-formula, conditional steps,
  loop-until, loop-range) — expert counsel: fights the tool
- Cross-tool parity research for slash commands — skill form chosen, moot
- Command form of the RALF skills — both stay as skills
- Formula-level numeric defaults for `MAX_ITERATIONS`
- Per-cycle audit beads — molecule state + skill's final report suffice
- Upgrading the formula completion-gate `code-review` step to use `ralf-review`
  (potential future improvement; not in this bead's scope — file as a
  follow-on bead if desired)

## Open questions

None remaining. (Prior OQ1 resolved: `bd comments add` is supported, mandated
in R6. Prior OQ2 resolved: default values are skill-rewrite decisions, not a
spec decision. Prior OQ3 resolved: `ralf:cycles=N` without `ralf:required` is
a no-op — formula step only dispatches when `ralf:required` is present.
Duplicate-label concern resolved by R15 setter contract.)

## Design notes

- **Skill split** because review and implement are distinct concerns that
  currently share a body only because the original skill grew that way.
  Separating them yields clean consumers: `brainstorm-bead.ralf-spec-review`
  dispatches `ralf-review`; `implement-feature.implement` dispatches
  `ralf-implement` when labeled.
- **Worktree responsibility OUT of both skills** because worktree/isolation
  is a caller concern, not an inner-methodology concern. Bead formulas own
  their worktree step; standalone users own their own checkout state.
- **Single `ralf:required` label, step-type routes** because the formula
  step already knows whether it's an implement-type or review-type step.
  Separate labels would be redundant disambiguation.
- **Setter-enforced deduplication (R15)** because beads labels are strings —
  `bd label add` does NOT overwrite like key=value. Pushing the complexity to
  the setter keeps the consumer contract simple.
- **Label over variable** because labels are queryable (`bd label list`,
  `bd ready --label`), survive compaction, can be set/adjusted manually,
  and integrate with the existing lifecycle-label pattern.
- **Signal set during `brainstorm-bead.finalize`** because that's when the
  spec is assessed — pushing the decision to dispatch time invites per-agent
  prose-parsing drift.
- **Skills own their defaults** because `MAX_ITERATIONS` is intrinsic to the
  RALF mechanic, not to the workflow it runs in.
- **Audit via `bd comments add`**, NOT `bd update --notes`. Reason: `--notes`
  REPLACES notes (`brainstorm-bead.formula.toml:122` convention holds the spec).
- **Context-detection contract (R4)** is deterministic: bead-ID argument +
  `bd show` success. No worktree detection, no magic.

title: Divergence 3: clarify delivery authorization boundaries + beads-aware delivery overlay
description:
# Context

Divergence 3 cascade from the discord-agent-scaffold session audit (session `64012e00`). After RALF-IT implementation completed in a worktree, the agent said *"Branch is ready for delivery. Next step is `finishing-a-development-branch` (PR). Ready when you are."* and stopped. No PR was created, no push happened, no bead close.

Agent's self-diagnosis: *"My over-learned caution around delivery actions overrode an explicit skill instruction. The RALF-IT skill says REQUIRED but I treated user confirmation as a prerequisite for even initiating the delivery workflow."*

Primary cascade protection lives in Divergences 1 and 2 — if the molecule runs end-to-end and formulas drive delivery (via lu3.2's delivery step citation), agents don't stand at a "ready when you are" moment. This bead closes the remaining defense-in-depth gaps in core rules and adds a bead-specific delivery overlay.

# Root causes addressed

1. **Asymmetric authorization language** — rules emphasize "merge requires authorization" heavily; never explicitly say "PR creation is AUTOMATIC"
2. **No action categorization** — AUTOMATIC vs AUTHORIZATION-REQUIRED is implicit, agents over-generalize caution
3. **"Ready when you are" pattern not flagged** — reads as courteous, is actually stopping work mid-workflow
4. **Duplicate PR comments block** in current `delivery.md` (minor housekeeping)

Explicitly OUT of scope:
- ralf-it skill cleanup (captured separately — see ralf-it re-evaluation bead)
- Formula delivery step citations (moved to lu3.2 scope)

# Edits required

## Core rules (beads-agnostic — `src/user/.claude/rules/`)

### `delivery.md`

Add "Action Categorization" section:

    ## Action Categories
    
    **AUTOMATIC** — execute without user authorization:
    - Create worktree
    - Commit on feature branch
    - Push feature branch
    - Create PR
    - Invoke wait-for-pr-comments
    - Apply unambiguous PR feedback
    
    **REQUIRES EXPLICIT AUTHORIZATION**:
    - Merging a PR ("go ahead and merge", "ship it", etc.)
    - Force-pushing
    - Destructive git operations (reset --hard, clean -fd, branch -D)
    - Pushing directly to main/master
    
    Creating a PR is NOT authorization to merge. But PR creation itself is AUTOMATIC — do not pause.

Add red flag:

    **Red flag**: "Ready when you are" / "ready for delivery" / "awaiting your go-ahead" BEFORE PR creation → STOP. Delivery is automatic. Execute `finishing-a-development-branch` now. Only pause AT the merge step.

De-dupe the duplicate "PR comments" block (lines 15-18 and 23 currently repeat the same instruction).

### `completion-gate.md`

Sharpen HARD STOP from:
> **HARD STOP**: After this gate, you MUST run delivery steps before calling work complete.
> DO NOT commit to main. DO NOT push directly. `finishing-a-development-branch` is NEXT.

To:
> **HARD STOP**: After this gate, AUTOMATICALLY execute delivery steps. Do NOT pause for authorization.
> DO NOT commit to main. DO NOT push directly.
>
> Execute IN ORDER, without asking: (1) worktree if not already in one, (2) `finishing-a-development-branch`, (3) `wait-for-pr-comments`. Only pause at the merge step.

## Beads overlay (NEW file — `src/plugins/beads/.claude/rules/delivery.md`)

New file, auto-appends to core `delivery.md` when beads plugin is installed (per the `rules/*.md` append-on-collision install behavior).

Content:

    # Delivery (beads-aware addendum)
    
    For bead-tracked work, delivery runs inside formula steps, not as a peer workflow:
    
    - `implement-feature.formula.toml` delivery step invokes `finishing-a-development-branch`
    - `merge-and-cleanup.formula.toml` handles merge after explicit user authorization
    
    Do NOT invoke `finishing-a-development-branch` as a peer of the bead workflow — it runs INSIDE the formula step.
    
    If you arrive at the end of a formula step and are uncertain whether delivery has run, check via `bd show <bead-id>` and `bd mol current <mol-id>` — the molecule's next step drives the action, not the agent's judgment.

# Design decisions

- **Core rules are beads-agnostic**: action categorization, "ready when you are" red flag, HARD STOP sharpening apply universally. They help even without beads installed.
- **Beads overlay via rules file append**: install script's `rules/*.md` append-on-collision behavior lets us add beads-aware delivery guidance without duplicating or fragmenting the core rules.
- **Ralf-it explicitly NOT touched here**: its re-evaluation is a separate, larger design question captured in its own bead.
- **Formula delivery step citation belongs in lu3.2**: that's the partnership-contract's formula-citation pattern, not a core rules change.

# Acceptance criteria

- `src/user/.claude/rules/delivery.md` has "Action Categories" block with AUTOMATIC / AUTHORIZATION-REQUIRED lists
- `src/user/.claude/rules/delivery.md` has the "Ready when you are" red flag
- Duplicate PR comments block in `src/user/.claude/rules/delivery.md` is de-duped (one instance, not two)
- `src/user/.claude/rules/completion-gate.md` HARD STOP says "AUTOMATICALLY execute delivery steps. Do NOT pause for authorization. Only pause AT the merge step."
- NEW file exists: `src/plugins/beads/.claude/rules/delivery.md` with bead-specific delivery addendum
- Install script correctly appends the overlay to the installed `~/.claude/rules/delivery.md` when beads is present (verify with a dry-run install)
- Build passes. Typecheck passes. Tests pass.

# Notes

- Divergence 3 fix depends minimally on lu3.1/lu3.2 landing — core rules changes stand alone, but the beads overlay is most effective once formulas drive delivery (lu3.2)
- Discovered during session `c06c7b55` analysis of discord-agent-scaffold session `64012e00`
close_reason: Action categories + Ready-when-you-are red flag + HARD STOP sharpened + beads delivery overlay (verified via dry-run)

title: Divergence 2: beads ↔ superpowers partnership contract (overlay rules)
description:
# Context

Divergence 2 cascade from the discord-agent-scaffold session audit (session `64012e00`). After the brainstorm molecule was abandoned in Divergence 1, the agent fell back to `superpowers:brainstorming` standalone, then naturally followed the superpowers chain: `writing-plans` → two-options menu → `subagent-driven-development`. `implement-bead` was never invoked, `implementation-ready` label never set.

Reframed: this is not "agent chose wrong menu option." It's "agent was pulled by skills that compete with the bead lifecycle because there is no explicit boundary between the two paradigms."

# Strategy: establish a partnership contract

**Beads = OUTER lifecycle** (what work exists, state, dependencies, multi-session persistence).
**Superpowers = INNER methodology** (how to actually do the work at each step).

`brainstorm-bead.formula.toml` already models this correctly — its `discuss` step invokes `superpowers:brainstorming`. All formulas should follow that pattern. Skills that duplicate bead lifecycle responsibilities are fenced off for bead-tracked work.

## Inner methodology skills (partners — use freely)

- `superpowers:brainstorming`
- `superpowers:systematic-debugging`
- `superpowers:root-cause-tracing`
- `superpowers:test-driven-development`
- `superpowers:verification-before-completion`
- `superpowers:using-git-worktrees`
- `superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch`
- `superpowers:requesting-code-review`
- `superpowers:receiving-code-review`
- `superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents`

## Off-limits for bead-tracked work (compete with bead lifecycle)

- `superpowers:writing-plans` → the bead description IS the plan
- `superpowers:executing-plans` → `implement-bead` is the executor
- `superpowers:subagent-driven-development` → `implement-bead` orchestrates via formula DAG

# Edits required

## `src/plugins/beads/.claude/rules/beads.md`

Add a new top-level section "Skill Partnership" with the two lists above and a short rule: "If you find yourself matching one of the off-limits skills while on a bead, STOP. The right path is `start-bead` → `brainstorm-bead` → `implement-bead`."

## `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/start-bead/SKILL.md`

Add a Red Flags row:
> | "After brainstorming, I should invoke writing-plans next" | No. Bead is the plan. Next is `implement-bead`. |

Plus a short recovery paragraph: "If you end up at `writing-plans`' two-options menu while on a bead, STOP. Set labels if missing, then `implement-bead`. Do not pick either menu option."

## `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/implement-bead/SKILL.md`

Add a Red Flags row:
> | "I'll just invoke ralf-it / subagent-driven-development / executing-plans directly" | No. `implement-bead` pours a formula which orchestrates those methodology skills INSIDE formula steps. |

## `src/plugins/beads/.beads/formulas/*.formula.toml`

Each formula step that embeds a methodology skill should cite it explicitly in the step description — same pattern as `brainstorm-bead`'s `discuss` step (`Skill: superpowers:brainstorming`). Files to update:
- `implement-feature.formula.toml` — tests step → TDD; verify step → verification-before-completion; isolate step → using-git-worktrees
- `fix-bug.formula.toml` — diagnose step → systematic-debugging; root-cause step → root-cause-tracing
- `merge-and-cleanup.formula.toml` — deliver step → finishing-a-development-branch; review step → requesting-code-review

(Exact list of step edits depends on each formula's current structure — determined during implementation.)

# Design decisions

- **Option 1 (overlay rules) chosen for phase 1**: zero maintenance, upstream superpowers fixes flow in, no fork management. If drift persists in future sessions, escalate per the backlog bead "Phase 2: fork superpowers → beadpowers plugin."
- **Fence, don't replace**: off-limits skills are still available for non-bead work. The rules scope the ban to "bead-tracked work."
- **Explicit skill citations in formulas**: turns the formula into the outer loop and makes the methodology skill embedding unambiguous. Same pattern brainstorm-bead already uses.
- **No new skills created**: all behavior changes are documentation / rule additions.

# Acceptance criteria

- `beads.md` has a "Skill Partnership" section naming the inner tools and the three off-limits skills
- `start-bead` has a Red Flag row rejecting `writing-plans` for bead work plus a recovery paragraph
- `implement-bead` has a Red Flag row rejecting direct invocation of methodology skills as peers (vs. inside formula steps)
- Formula step descriptions for `implement-feature`, `fix-bug`, and `merge-and-cleanup` cite the superpowers methodology skills they embed (following `brainstorm-bead` discuss step pattern)
- Manual verification: audit a future session — agent on a bead does not invoke `writing-plans`, `executing-plans`, or `subagent-driven-development` as peers of the bead workflow
- Build passes. Typecheck passes. Tests pass.

# Notes

- Related to `agents-config-lu3.1` (wisp materialization) — partnership contract is most effective once the molecule actually runs end-to-end
- The fork-and-modify escalation path is tracked separately as a P3 backlog bead (not starting unless overlay rules prove insufficient)
- Reframing credit: analysis conversation in session `c06c7b55` identified the skill-paradigm conflict as the root cause rather than individual menu/label fixes
notes:
SCOPE ADDENDUM (session c06c7b55):

Include explicit delivery step in `implement-feature.formula.toml`. The formula needs a delivery step that cites `superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch` — this completes the partnership contract's formula-citation pattern at the delivery boundary.

Without it, agents running `implement-feature` (via `implement-bead`) reach the end of the "implement" step and have no formula-embedded signal that delivery is automatic. They drift to "ready when you are" (Divergence 3 pattern).

Moved from original Divergence 3 scope (lu3.3) to consolidate formula methodology citations under lu3.2's partnership-contract theme.

Updated acceptance criterion: `implement-feature.formula.toml` has a dedicated delivery step that cites `superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch`, following the same pattern as `brainstorm-bead`'s `discuss` step citing `superpowers:brainstorming`.
close_reason: Skill Partnership section + Red Flags in start-bead & implement-bead + formula skill citations

title: Revisit wait-for-pr-comments / resolve-pr-comments against beads workflow system (formulas, molecules, wisps)
description: The two PR review skills (wait-for-pr-comments, resolve-pr-comments) were designed as standalone Claude skills. But this project has a richer bead-driven workflow substrate: formulas (workflow templates), molecules (structured DAG executions poured from formulas), and wisps (ephemeral state store). Question: should PR review response be a formula/molecule instead of (or in addition to) free-standing skills? Benefits to evaluate: (a) persistent DAG state across session compaction, (b) visible progress via bd mol status, (c) alignment with the brainstorm-bead / implement-bead / run-queue pipeline, (d) a per-comment step ledger instead of transient subagent reports. Costs: duplication with existing skill content, formula-authoring overhead, need to pick the right granularity (one molecule per PR? one per comment?). Deliverable: design doc / spec decision — formula yes/no, and if yes, the DAG shape. Implementation is a follow-up bead if we say yes.
notes:
SCOPE ADDENDUM (session eed3f46f, 2026-04-19):

Beyond the 'formula vs standalone skill' question, this bead should also re-think the BOUNDARIES and INTERPLAY between wait-for-pr-comments and resolve-pr-comments. Observed problem during lu3 PR #11 session: resolve-pr-comments was never proactively invoked even though it owned the exact know-how I needed (review-thread reply endpoint, resolveReviewThread GraphQL mutation, reply-only-dont-resolve-on-skip semantics).

Root causes:

1. **Hand-off trigger too narrow.** resolve-pr-comments frontmatter says 'Hand-off target from wait-for-pr-comments when it reports skipped or ambiguous items.' When wait-for-pr-comments mechanically auto-fixes everything inline, it still leaves threads unreplied and unresolved on GitHub — but there's no skipped-items trigger, so resolve-pr-comments never activates. The closing-out workflow is orphaned.

2. **Keyword gaps in frontmatter.** The description emphasizes 'addressing' / 'fixing' comments, not 'reply', 'resolve', 'thread', 'post fix confirmation'. User intents like 'reply to Copilot' or 'resolve the threads' don't pattern-match to the skill name.

3. **Overlapping responsibilities, unclear ownership.** wait-for-pr-comments does auto-fix + commit + push + Phase 5 'report to user' — but then leaves the GitHub-side reply/resolve housekeeping undone. resolve-pr-comments has the full reply/resolve Phase 5, but its Phase 3 subagent-per-fix workflow duplicates wait-for-pr-comments' Mechanical bucket. Neither skill cleanly owns: (a) post-fix reply-and-resolve bookkeeping, (b) rebuttal/skipped-reply bookkeeping, (c) mixed bags where some items are auto-fixed and others need judgment.

Proposed boundary redesign to evaluate:

Option A — wait-for-pr-comments as detector only:
  wait-for-pr-comments ONLY polls + reports. All fixing, replying, and resolving
  lives in resolve-pr-comments. Simpler, but loses the zero-token quick-win for
  trivial typos.

Option B — split by feedback lifecycle phase:
  wait-for-pr-comments owns ARRIVAL (poll, detect, trivial inline auto-fix +
  commit/push). resolve-pr-comments ALWAYS runs after (whether fully-auto-fixed
  or with leftovers) to do reply + resolve housekeeping. Cleanest division
  but requires the hand-off trigger to fire unconditionally.

Option C — unified PR-response formula/molecule:
  Consolidate both skills into a single formula (ties in with bl3's original
  formula/molecule question). Molecule steps: (1) detect + poll Copilot, (2)
  classify + dispatch per-comment subagents, (3) push combined commit, (4)
  reply to every thread, (5) resolve FIXED threads, (6) report. State lives
  in the molecule, not transient skill reports.

Deliverable updates:
- design decision: skill-pair rethink (A/B/C) OR formula
- if skills: frontmatter rewrites with keyword coverage ('reply', 'resolve', 'thread', 'post fix confirmation', 'rebut')
- clearly-named hand-off trigger so the second skill fires unconditionally after the first
- explicit description of who owns Phase 5 reply + resolve
close_reason: Spec landed (docs/specs/2026-04-26-pr-review-skill-redesign.md) and implementation merged via PR #20.

title: wait-for-pr-comments: add final unresolved-threads check before closing await-review step
description: After Skill B (reply-and-resolve-pr-threads) completes, before closing the await-review molecule step, the skill should do a final GraphQL check for any non-resolved, non-outdated review threads. If any exist, loop back through classify → fix → reply. The 30-second re-review poll window in Phase 6 is insufficient — Copilot can submit a new review AFTER the window closes (demonstrated during 7bk.9 implementation). Final check should be: gh api graphql reviewThreads query, filter isResolved=false and isOutdated=false. If count > 0, treat as a new round of Phase 3.

title: Completion gate: code review
description:
Dispatch quality-reviewer subagent against ALL changed files.

Agent: quality-reviewer
Scope: every file changed since branch creation, reviewed against:
  - The implementation plan restated in the confirm step (per the bead spec)
  - SOLID and DRY principles
  - Security by design (OWASP top 10, input validation, no injection paths)
  - Architectural consistency with the rest of the codebase
  - The standards in INSTRUCTIONS.md

Address ALL findings before proceeding. If a finding is deferred,
document the rationale explicitly.

For high-stakes changes (security-sensitive, architecture shifts):
  /codex:adversarial-review --wait --model gpt-5.4

title: Confirm bead is brainstormed and restate the implementation plan
description:
Verify that bead {{bead-id}} has been through brainstorm-bead and that
the spec exists in the bead. NEVER invoke superpowers:brainstorming —
the bead description IS the plan.

Read bead and labels:

  bd show {{bead-id}}
  bd label list {{bead-id}}

== HAPPY PATH (both `brainstormed` AND `implementation-ready` labels present) ==

1. Read the bead description and acceptance criteria.
2. Restate the implementation plan in 1-3 sentences to prove understanding
   (e.g. "I will modify <files> to <do X> so that <AC pass>").
3. Mark this step done. The next step (worktree) runs.

DO NOT call `bd update {{bead-id}} --acceptance` or `--notes` —
brainstorm-bead already wrote the spec; rewriting it would destroy it.

== MISSING-LABEL PATH (either label absent) ==

Aborting from either branch below means: leave this `confirm` step
OPEN (do not close it), do NOT call `bd mol burn` from within this
step, and print this one-line follow-up:

  Molecule paused at confirm. Run brainstorm-bead on this bead and re-attempt, or `bd mol burn <mol-id>` to discard.

Leave the disposition (re-attempt or burn) to the operator (or to
run-queue policy).

Detect execution mode from your own dispatch context:

- AUTONOMOUS mode (you were dispatched by run-queue, or any
  context where no human is at the keyboard):
  - Escalate the bead so a human can address it:
      bd label add {{bead-id}} human
      bd update {{bead-id}} --append-notes "missing brainstormed/implementation-ready labels; recommend running brainstorm-bead before implementation."
    (NOT `bd human <id>` — that's the help command, not a label applier.)
  - Do NOT prompt — there is no operator to answer.
  - Then abort (per the shared abort mechanism above).

- INTERACTIVE mode (start-bead routed you here, or a human ran
  `bd mol pour` directly and is in conversation with you):
  - Print a clear warning: "Bead {{bead-id}} is missing brainstormed/implementation-ready labels — it has not been through brainstorm-bead. Implementation may run on an incomplete spec."
  - Ask the user:
    (a) Abort and route the bead through start-bead/brainstorm-bead first.
    (b) Proceed with implementation using whatever is in the bead now.
  - On (a): abort (per the shared abort mechanism above).
  - On (b): proceed to worktree.
close_reason: Confirmed: brainstormed + implementation-ready labels present. Plan restated.

title: Housekeeping: close, remember, file discovered work
description:
Complete the lifecycle and preserve context for future sessions.

1. Close the bead:
     bd close {{bead-id}} --reason "Implemented and PR merged"

2. Walk the parent chain; close each ancestor epic whose remaining
   children are all closed (stops at the first ancestor that still
   has any non-closed children, or when there is no parent):

     PARENT=$(bd show {{bead-id}} --json | jq -r '.[0].parent // empty')
     while [ -n "$PARENT" ]; do
       NON_CLOSED=$(bd list --parent="$PARENT" --json | jq '[.[] | select(.status != "closed")] | length')
       [ "$NON_CLOSED" = "0" ] || break
       bd close "$PARENT" --reason "All children closed"
       PARENT=$(bd show "$PARENT" --json | jq -r '.[0].parent // empty')
     done

3. Record any non-obvious decisions or hard-won insights:
     bd remember "insight about this system or pattern"
     bd decision record --title="Why we chose X over Y"

4. File any work discovered during implementation. Prefer epic-sibling
   placement over orphan-with-dep:

     PARENT=$(bd show {{bead-id}} --json | jq -r '.[0].parent // empty')

     # Decide placement via the sibling test (see rules/beads.md I3):
     # would this work have been on the parent epic's original plan, if
     # we'd thought of it? Yes → sibling. No → orphan + discovered-from.
     IS_SIBLING_SUBTASK=false  # set to true only when the answer is yes

     if [ -n "$PARENT" ] && [ "$IS_SIBLING_SUBTASK" = true ]; then
       # Create INSIDE the parent epic as a sibling:
       bd create "Found: ..." -t "<type>" -p "<prio>" --parent "$PARENT"
     else
       # Otherwise create as an orphan and link with discovered-from:
       NEW=$(bd create "Found: ..." -t "<type>" -p "<prio>" --json | jq -r '.id')
       bd dep add "$NEW" {{bead-id}} --type discovered-from
     fi

   Sibling test (see rules/beads.md I3): would this discovered work
   have been on the parent epic's original plan, if we'd thought of
   it? Yes → sibling (--parent). No → orphan + discovered-from.

5. Burn this wisp (if running as vapor):
     bd mol burn <this-mol-id>
close_reason: Housekeeping: discovered work filed as 7bk.17 and 7bk.18, key memories saved.

title: Implement Per-step claude -p orchestration: redesign bead pipeline (replace run-queue skill, rewrite implement-bead, define 7-stage feature workflow) (green phase)

Run tests after each meaningful change:

This step is complete only when all tests are GREEN.

title: Create pull request

Skill: superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch

title: Completion gate: verify with evidence
description:
Run the full verification suite. Evidence is required — no claims without output.

Skill: superpowers:verification-before-completion

Required gates — show the actual command output for each:
  [ ] Tests pass        → run test suite, paste output
  [ ] Build succeeds    → run build, paste output
  [ ] Lint clean        → run linter, paste output

Do NOT mark this step complete unless all four gates show clean output.

title: Write failing tests (red phase)
description:
Write tests FIRST. No implementation code at this step.

Skills: superpowers:test-driven-development, superpowers:writing-unit-tests, superpowers:testing-anti-patterns

Rules:
- Tests must be written against the acceptance criteria on the bead (set by brainstorm-bead, restated in the confirm step)
- Cover: happy path, edge cases, error paths
- Do NOT mock what you have not verified you understand
- Do NOT write test-only methods in production code

Run the tests and confirm they FAIL. Failure is correct here.

  go test ./...           # Go projects
  pnpm test               # Node/TypeScript projects
  pytest                  # Python projects

If a test passes before any implementation, either the test is wrong
or the feature already exists. Investigate before continuing.

title: Completion gate: simplify
description:
Invoke the `simplify` skill against changed code.

Skill: simplify

This step exists because the implementing agent always misses
at least one DRY violation or clarity issue. It is not optional.

title: Gemini agent loader rejects shared agent frontmatter (tools/skills/color/memory)
notes:
Repro: Install user-scope content via scripts/install.sh with Gemini detected, then start gemini.

Actual: Gemini fails to load three agents with validation errors:

  ~/.gemini/agents/bead-verifier.md
    - tools: Expected array, received string
    - Unrecognized keys: 'skills', 'color'

  ~/.gemini/agents/quality-reviewer.md
    - tools: Expected array, received string
    - Unrecognized keys: 'skills', 'memory', 'color'

  ~/.gemini/agents/tech-lead.md
    - Unrecognized key: 'color'

Expected: Agents installed to ~/.gemini/agents/ load cleanly under Gemini CLI's stricter schema validator.

Root cause (suspected): Shared agent files in src/user/.agents/agents/ use Claude-flavored frontmatter. The installer copies them verbatim to ~/.gemini/agents/. Gemini's loader requires:
  - tools as a YAML array (not a comma-separated string)
  - no unrecognized keys (skills, color, memory are Claude-specific)

Fix options to consider during brainstorming:
  1. Tool-specific frontmatter transform during install (strip/convert per target)
  2. Normalize shared agent frontmatter to a lowest-common-denominator schema
  3. Maintain per-tool agent variants with shared body via partials

Affected files (initial scan target): src/user/.agents/agents/{bead-verifier,quality-reviewer,tech-lead}.md and possibly other shared agents not yet exercised by Gemini.

title: Single-context mode for implement-bead (alternative to per-stage claude -p)
description:
ONE agent runs the entire molecule in ONE session, no per-stage `claude -p` invocations. Proposes an alternate operational model that is mutually exclusive with 7bk.9's per-stage `claude -p` architecture.

Full design (current target): `docs/specs/bead-pipeline-architecture.md` §10 (Alternative architectures).

## Why this exists as a sibling exploration (not in epic 7bk)

The agent-fleet epic (7bk) commits to specialized agents dispatched per stage, which under current Claude Code constraints requires the per-stage `claude -p` model from 7bk.9. This bead asks the orthogonal question: should we instead run a single long-lived session for the whole molecule?

Trade-offs to evaluate during brainstorm:
- Bounded vs unbounded context: single-context bounds at one session; per-stage bounds at one stage
- Subagent-spawn constraint: single-context cannot dispatch subagents from within subagents (Claude Code limit). Per-stage works around this by exiting + respawning
- Performance: single-context avoids `claude -p` cold-start tax (~5-30s × 8 stages). Per-stage costs that tax but allows parallelism across beads
- Resilience: per-stage idempotent re-entry handles crashes; single-context loses everything on crash

## Open question for brainstorm

Is this:
- (a) A fallback profile for trivial beads where per-stage overhead exceeds benefit?
- (b) An A/B alternative for performance comparison?
- (c) An eventual replacement when subagent-spawning constraints relax in Claude Code?
- (d) An obsolete idea given 7bk.9 ships first?

The relationship to 7bk.9 must be made explicit before implementation.

## Dependencies

Soft dep on agents-config-7bk via `related-to` edge — bd CLI does not allow tasks to hard-block epics. The dependency is OPERATOR-RESPECTED BY CONVENTION: brainstorm of this bead should not start until 7bk's main per-stage architecture (7bk.9) has shipped, but `bd ready` does not exclude this bead automatically.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Brainstorm produces a decision among (a)/(b)/(c)/(d) above
- If implementing: spec details, formula variant, model + effort defaults
- Build passes. Typecheck passes. Tests pass.

title: RALF-IT: hard-fail on foreign-eyes degradation; record degradation status in PR
description:
Currently RALF-IT silently degrades when foreign-eyes (Codex, Gemini) are unavailable: "foreign-agent failure → degrade to pure fresh-eyes (iteration still counts)." Result: anti-bias property silently disappears; reviewers and operators have no visibility into degradation.

## Why

The architectural justification for foreign-eyes is anti-bias: a different model with different training catches Claude's blind spots. When foreign-eyes are unavailable for multiple iterations, RALF-IT becomes a self-review loop branded as adversarial. If shipped with this property invisibly disabled, the architecture's quality claim is unfounded for those beads.

Three failure modes to handle:
- Codex auth expired or quota exhausted
- Gemini quota exhausted or service unavailable
- Both unavailable concurrently (network incident, etc.)

## What changes

1. RALF-IT skill tracks foreign-eyes invocation outcomes per iteration (success / unavailable / errored).
2. After each iteration, persist degradation state to step-bead notes:
   `FOREIGN-EYES-ITER-<n>: codex=<status>, gemini=<status>`
3. At end of RALF loop:
   - If foreign-eyes were degraded in ≥2 of N iterations → flag-human; do NOT mark green-loop complete.
   - Stamp `foreign-eyes-degraded:<n>/<N>` label on the source bead.
4. PR description includes a foreign-eyes status section:
   - For each iteration: which foreign-eye ran successfully.
   - Aggregate degradation count.
   - If degradation occurred but did not trigger flag-human (only 1 of N), note this so reviewers know.

## Fallback options (out of scope; flag for future design)

- Cursor / direct ChatGPT API as a third foreign-eye option
- Local self-hosted model fallback

## Dependencies

None. Modifies the RALF-IT skill independently of the agent-fleet epic. NOT a child of the epic.

## Acceptance Criteria

- RALF-IT skill tracks per-iteration foreign-eye outcomes in step-bead notes
- Hard-fail trigger: ≥2 of N iterations degraded → flag-human + stamp foreign-eyes-degraded:<n>/<N> label
- PR description includes foreign-eyes status section (template added to superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch flow OR injected by RALF-IT)
- Smoke test: simulate Codex unavailable for iter 1 + Gemini unavailable for iter 2; verify flag-human fires and label is stamped
- Build passes. Typecheck passes. Tests pass.

title: Per-step Model: and Effort: directives in formula TOMLs
description:
Add support for per-step Model: and Effort: override directives in formula TOML files. These get applied at bd mol pour / bd mol wisp time (or read at orchestrator dispatch time, depending on bd's actual capabilities — see Open Question).

## Why

7bk.9's per-stage claude -p model needs a way to specify which model and effort drives each stage's orchestrator. Without this, every stage falls through to the same default — losing the "right-sized models" pillar of the agent-fleet epic.

The Tier 3 cost-rightsizing bead leans on this mechanism for at least 5 cost-rightsizing recommendations: per-stage opus vs sonnet, per-stage effort:high vs effort:medium, per-iteration vs final-pass model differentiation.

## Open Question (resolve at implementation)

Investigate whether bd formula TOML parser already supports Model: / Effort: directives in step bodies, OR whether bd needs a parser extension. If extension is needed, that becomes a bd-side dependency.

Likely resolution paths:
- (a) bd already supports them as free-form keys in step body, just need a convention.
- (b) bd needs a small parser change to recognize them as structured fields.
- (c) Apply at pour-time via post-processing: pour reads formula step body, extracts directives, stamps step-bead with model-<id> / effort-<level> labels.

## Behavior

- Formula step body specifies Model: <model-id> and Effort: <level> directives.
- bd mol pour / wisp processes them per the chosen mechanism above.
- Driver reads next-ready step-bead's labels (or notes, depending on mechanism) and spawns claude -p with the appropriate model and effort.
- No directive in formula step → agent's own frontmatter defaults apply.
- No source-bead-level override mechanism in initial scope. If a particular bead truly needs a non-formula model/effort, that is a manual edit / one-off concern — do not build vocabulary for it now.

## Out of Scope

- Per-source-bead override labels (no model-override-* / effort-override-* labels at this stage; formula-only)
- Model/effort proposals at brainstorm-time (classify-and-policy step does NOT include model/effort knobs)

## Dependencies

- agents-config-7bk.9 (the per-stage claude -p model that consumes these directives)

## Acceptance Criteria

- bd formula DSL supports per-step Model: and Effort: directives (or post-processing path documented)
- implement-feature.formula.toml + fix-bug.formula.toml updated with directives per Tier 3 recommendations (when Tier 3 lands)
- Smoke test: pour a formula with directives; verify step-beads carry the expected sizing; verify driver dispatches with the specified model/effort
- Build passes. Typecheck passes. Tests pass.

title: resolve-human-bead skill: safely resume work after human intervention on a flagged bead
description:
A new skill that helps a human bring a human-flagged bead back into the autonomous pipeline safely. The skill evaluates what the human has changed and proposes the right resumption path before re-claiming work.

Full design: `docs/specs/bead-pipeline-architecture.md` §7 (Skill catalog) and §5.6 (Human-flag protocol).

## Why

There is no automated mechanism to "un-humanize" a human-labeled bead. Human flags surface in `bd human list`; the human reviews, makes whatever change is needed (amend spec, file follow-up, fix tooling, decide to abandon), and then... what? Removing the `human` label naively allows the driver to re-claim the bead — but the persisted molecule state (RALF-IT-ITER markers, parked step-bead status, worktree-path label) may be incompatible with the human's changes.

Example: human-flagged bead had a contradictory AC. Human amends the spec (re-runs brainstorm or edits directly). Removing the human label and resuming RALF-IT at iteration n+1 would re-run logic against the changed AC with stale iteration count. Need the molecule burned + re-poured.

## What this skill does

1. Read the human-flagged bead's note + recent bd activity (commits, label changes, edits) to detect what the human did.
2. Re-evaluate current molecule state — what step is parked, what's been persisted (worktree, RALF-IT-ITER, PR URL, step-bead notes).
3. Diagnose the resume scenario:
   - Spec amended → recommend re-brainstorm (which invokes finalize's AC-reconciliation per architecture doc §4.3 to update follow-up children) + burn parked molecule
   - Scope expanded (new sibling work needed) → recommend filing follow-up bead with dep, then resume same molecule
   - Tooling/credential issue resolved → recommend resume same molecule, no reset
   - Architectural rework needed → recommend `bd mol squash` + re-pour fresh
   - Bead abandoned → recommend `bd close` with reason
   - Human follow-up verification → confirm the human verified the AC, prompt to apply `verified-by-human` label before closing the follow-up (per architecture doc §4.3)
4. Apply the recommended action with user confirmation: reset RALF-IT-ITER markers, burn molecules, remove human label, apply verified-by-human label on follow-ups, etc.

## Worktree fate (per architecture doc §5.6)

- Resume same molecule → keep worktree
- Burn + re-pour → delete worktree (and the worktree-path-* label on the molecule clears with the squash)
- Migration path is rare and operator-judgment; skill prompts before migrating

## Invocation paths

- Manual: `/resolve-human-bead <bead-id>` — human runs explicitly when ready
- Agent-detected: when user expresses intent to "resolve" / "fix" / "address" a bead carrying the human label, the agent routes here
- start-bead enhancement: start-bead recognizes when its target carries `human` and routes to resolve-human-bead (Route D, alongside existing routes for brainstorm / implement-ready / trivial inline)

## Dependencies

- agents-config-7bk.9 (per-stage claude -p architecture must be finalized; molecule-state semantics depend on it)

## Acceptance Criteria

- `src/plugins/beads/.agents/skills/resolve-human-bead/SKILL.md` exists with frontmatter and body matching spec
- `/resolve-human-bead` slash command at `src/plugins/beads/.claude/commands/resolve-human-bead.md`
- start-bead skill updated with Route D routing logic for human-labeled beads
- Skill recognizes the six resume scenarios (spec-amended, scope-expanded, tooling-resolved, architectural-rework, abandoned, human-followup-verification) and prompts the human appropriately for each
- For human-followup-verification scenario: skill prompts to apply `verified-by-human` label before closing the follow-up
- For spec-amended scenario: skill recommends re-brainstorm (which invokes idempotent finalize per architecture doc §4.3)
- End-to-end smoke 1: human-flagged bead with stale RALF-IT-ITER + amended spec; run /resolve-human-bead; skill detects spec change, recommends molecule burn + re-pour; molecule cleaned up correctly
- End-to-end smoke 2: human-flagged follow-up bead; run /resolve-human-bead; skill recognizes human-followup-verification; prompts for `verified-by-human` before close
- Build passes. Typecheck passes. Tests pass.

title: Confirm bead is brainstormed and restate the implementation plan
description:
Verify that bead {{bead-id}} has been through brainstorm-bead and that
the spec exists in the bead. NEVER invoke superpowers:brainstorming —
the bead description IS the plan.

Read bead and labels:

  bd show {{bead-id}}
  bd label list {{bead-id}}

== HAPPY PATH (both `brainstormed` AND `implementation-ready` labels present) ==

1. Read the bead description and acceptance criteria.
2. Restate the implementation plan in 1-3 sentences to prove understanding
   (e.g. "I will modify <files> to <do X> so that <AC pass>").
3. Mark this step done. The next step (worktree) runs.

DO NOT call `bd update {{bead-id}} --acceptance` or `--notes` —
brainstorm-bead already wrote the spec; rewriting it would destroy it.

== MISSING-LABEL PATH (either label absent) ==

Aborting from either branch below means: leave this `confirm` step
OPEN (do not close it), do NOT call `bd mol burn` from within this
step, and print this one-line follow-up:

  Molecule paused at confirm. Run brainstorm-bead on this bead and re-attempt, or `bd mol burn <mol-id>` to discard.

Leave the disposition (re-attempt or burn) to the operator (or to
run-queue policy).

Detect execution mode from your own dispatch context:

- AUTONOMOUS mode (you were dispatched by run-queue, or any
  context where no human is at the keyboard):
  - Escalate the bead so a human can address it:
      bd human {{bead-id}}
  - Note in escalation: "missing brainstormed/implementation-ready labels; recommend running brainstorm-bead before implementation."
  - Do NOT prompt — there is no operator to answer.
  - Then abort (per the shared abort mechanism above).

- INTERACTIVE mode (start-bead routed you here, or a human ran
  `bd mol pour` directly and is in conversation with you):
  - Print a clear warning: "Bead {{bead-id}} is missing brainstormed/implementation-ready labels — it has not been through brainstorm-bead. Implementation may run on an incomplete spec."
  - Ask the user:
    (a) Abort and route the bead through start-bead/brainstorm-bead first.
    (b) Proceed with implementation using whatever is in the bead now.
  - On (a): abort (per the shared abort mechanism above).
  - On (b): proceed to worktree.

title: Housekeeping: close, remember, file discovered work
description:
Complete the lifecycle and preserve context for future sessions.

1. Close the bead:
     bd close {{bead-id}} --reason "Implemented and PR merged"

2. Walk the parent chain; close each ancestor epic whose remaining
   children are all closed (stops at the first ancestor that still
   has any non-closed children, or when there is no parent):

     PARENT=$(bd show {{bead-id}} --json | jq -r '.[0].parent // empty')
     while [ -n "$PARENT" ]; do
       NON_CLOSED=$(bd list --parent="$PARENT" --json | jq '[.[] | select(.status != "closed")] | length')
       [ "$NON_CLOSED" = "0" ] || break
       bd close "$PARENT" --reason "All children closed"
       PARENT=$(bd show "$PARENT" --json | jq -r '.[0].parent // empty')
     done

3. Record any non-obvious decisions or hard-won insights:
     bd remember "insight about this system or pattern"
     bd decision record --title="Why we chose X over Y"

4. File any work discovered during implementation. Prefer epic-sibling
   placement over orphan-with-dep:

     PARENT=$(bd show {{bead-id}} --json | jq -r '.[0].parent // empty')

     # Decide placement via the sibling test (see rules/beads.md I3):
     # would this work have been on the parent epic's original plan, if
     # we'd thought of it? Yes → sibling. No → orphan + discovered-from.
     IS_SIBLING_SUBTASK=false  # set to true only when the answer is yes

     if [ -n "$PARENT" ] && [ "$IS_SIBLING_SUBTASK" = true ]; then
       # Create INSIDE the parent epic as a sibling:
       bd create "Found: ..." -t "<type>" -p "<prio>" --parent "$PARENT"
     else
       # Otherwise create as an orphan and link with discovered-from:
       NEW=$(bd create "Found: ..." -t "<type>" -p "<prio>" --json | jq -r '.id')
       bd dep add "$NEW" {{bead-id}} --type discovered-from
     fi

   Sibling test (see rules/beads.md I3): would this discovered work
   have been on the parent epic's original plan, if we'd thought of
   it? Yes → sibling (--parent). No → orphan + discovered-from.

5. Burn this wisp (if running as vapor):
     bd mol burn <this-mol-id>
